tempura.history: Batter-frying landed with those Portuguese missionaries, but tempura as we know it was made in Edo (old Tokyo): riverside yatai stalls fried skewered fish to order as fast, hot street food. Only in the 20th century did it climb to the refined counter and the kaiseki course, where a master fries each piece in sequence so it reaches you at its peak.
tempura.culture: It spans the whole social range of Japanese eating — a ¥200 stand-up snack, a neighbourhood teishoku, and a hushed ¥30,000 counter where you eat one piece at a time. In Kyoto it leans seasonal and vegetable-forward, a quiet showcase rather than a heavy fry-up.
tempura.gf: Wheat batter and wheat-soy tentsuyu — not GF. Ask for salt; rice-flour batter is rare.
tempura.vegan: Vegetable (yasai) tempura can be vegan if the batter has no egg and the dip isn't fish-dashi.
tempura.name: The word most likely comes from Latin/Portuguese tempora (têmporas) — the Catholic 'Ember Days' of fasting, when the faithful ate fish fried instead of meat. Portuguese missionaries brought both the technique and, in its name, a memory of Iberian fasting to 16th-century Nagasaki.
tempura.taste: The coating is not there for crunch — it's a vehicle. At its best the batter is thin, pale and lacy, almost dry, a veil that steams the seafood or vegetable inside so it emerges just-set and sweet rather than sealed in a thick shell. Look for pale colour (never deep golden), no oil slick on the paper, and the ingredient's own moisture intact. The salt or tentsuyu is a frame; the point is what the batter protected.

tonkatsu.name: Katsu is a battle-scarred loanword: French côtelette became English ‘cutlet,’ which Meiji Japan rendered katsuretsu (カツレツ), then clipped to katsu and bolted to ton (豚, ‘pig’). The full pedigree runs pōku katsuretto → pōku katsuretsu → buta-katsuretsu → tonkatsu, the contraction not standardizing until the late 1950s.
tonkatsu.history: By the standard account, the pork côtelette was born at Rengatei, the Ginza yōshoku house founded in 1895, which around 1899 listed a ‘pōku katsuretsu’ — swapping veal for cheaper pork and, crucially, abandoning the shallow pan-fry of French cookery for the deep, tempura-style immersion in oil that defines the dish (though several post-2017 studies dispute Rengatei’s claim to have invented it). The thick, fully deep-fried slab we now picture is younger, credited to Shimada Shinjirō of Ponchi-ken in Ueno around 1929, who fried a 2.5–3 cm cut through and plated it Japanese-style with rice, miso soup, and pickles.
tonkatsu.culture: Tonkatsu is the keystone of wafū yōshoku — a Western idea so thoroughly naturalized it now anchors comfort-food rituals, eaten with chopsticks and a katsu-bowl spawning katsudon, katsukarē, and the lucky-charm bowl gamed before exams because katsu puns on ‘to win.’ The table choreography is fixed: you grind your own white sesame in a ridged suribachi, fold in the thick Worcester-style sauce, and bury the cutlet in a snowdrift of finely shredded raw cabbage. Kyoto sends its pork south for Kagoshima kurobuta and prizes the city’s own specialists who treat the cut like kaiseki.
tonkatsu.taste: The tell of a great cutlet is the crust: shattering, pale-gold, standing slightly proud of the meat rather than welded to it in a greasy sheath — the gap is what the low-temperature, double-fry technique buys you. Look at the cut face: rōsu should show a clean rim of fat rendered to silk, not chewy rope, while leaner hire trades that richness for a fine, tender grain; either way the interior should read faintly blush and run clear juice, never gray and weeping oil. Drag a piece through fresh-ground sesame and you should taste the nuttiness lift the sweet-sour sauce rather than drown in it.
tonkatsu.gf: The panko crust and the dredging flour are wheat, the Worcester-style sauce is typically wheat-based, and tonkatsu sauce and many dipping sauces hide soy sauce — so the gluten is built into the dish itself; ask.
tonkatsu.vegan: It is pork by definition, the egg-and-flour bind is animal, and the cabbage’s likely dressing plus the sauce can carry dashi or anchovy — not vegan; ask.

ramen.name: The word arrived before the dish settled: Japanese first called these Chinese noodle soups Nankin soba (‘Nanjing noodles’), then shina soba, and after the war chūka soba (‘Chinese noodles’) once shina turned pejorative. ‘Ramen’ itself is generally traced to the Chinese lāmiàn (拉麵, ‘pulled noodles’), though scholars caution the bowl owes little to actual hand-pulled lamian — the name stuck, the lineage is murkier.
ramen.history: Ramen is Japan’s most beloved Chinese import: Chinese cooks in the treaty-port Chinatowns of Yokohama, Kobe, Nagasaki and Hakodate were ladling out wheat-noodle soups by the 1880s, and a 1910 Asakusa shop, Rairaiken, is often cited as the first to plate the dish for a Japanese crowd. The true explosion came after 1945, when American wheat aid and returning soldiers turned a niche street food into a national staple. A separate thread runs alongside the shops: in 1958 Momofuku Ando flash-fried noodles into Nissin’s Chikin Ramen, inventing instant ramen and eventually carrying the word to the whole planet.
ramen.culture: No other dish maps Japan so finely — nearly every prefecture and city claims a signature style, and a great bowl is judged with the seriousness of wine, down to noodle gauge and the angle of the chāshū. Ramen is fast food and craft obsession at once: slurped alone at a counter in minutes, yet chased across the country by hobbyists logging shops like pilgrimages, and immortalized in a whole genre of films and manga.
ramen.taste: Think in four parts and how they lock together: the broth (dashi/stock), the tare (the salty-umami seasoning sauce pooled at the bottom), the kansui noodle whose alkaline bite gives that yellow, springy slip, and the aroma oil slicked on top. A great bowl is balance — tare not drowning the stock, noodle thickness matched to broth body (thin and straight for clear shoyu, thicker and wavy for heavy miso or tonkotsu), and a near-scalding temperature that releases the aroma oil. Eat fast: the noodles keep cooking in the bowl, and a soggy strand is a ruined bowl, so taste the soup, then attack the noodles before they soften.
ramen.gf: Ramen is built on wheat — the kansui noodle is the dish, the tare and broth often carry soy sauce, and the standard toppings (menma seasoning, fish cakes) can hide wheat too; even a ‘gluten-free’ rice-noodle bowl needs the tare and stock checked, so ask.
ramen.vegan: Most broths lean on pork, chicken or katsuobushi/niboshi (bonito and sardine) plus a tare cut with soy and sometimes fish — genuine vegan bowls exist but are a deliberate specialty, never the default.

katsu_don.history: A Taishō-era refinement of plain tonkatsu; one popular account credits a Waseda student around 1921 with simmering the cutlet briefly with onion and egg. It turned a Western-style fried cutlet into a thoroughly Japanese donburi.
katsu_don.culture: The 'win' pun is alive and well — students really do eat it the night before exams. It's hearty, cheap, and emotionally loaded in a way few rice bowls are.
katsu_don.gf: Wheat panko, soy-based sauce — not GF.
katsu_don.name: A pork cutlet sliced over onion and egg on rice — but the name carries a pun that made it a ritual. 'Katsu' (cutlet, from the English 'cutlet') is a homophone of 勝つ, 'to win.' So katsudon became Japan's good-luck meal: eaten before exams and matches, and — a fixture of every cop drama — slid across the table to a suspect in the interrogation room.
katsu_don.taste: The whole pleasure is a tension between crisp and soft: the panko crust should keep some bite where it meets the egg even as its underside drinks the sauce, the egg should be half-set, and the cutlet's fat rendered. A great katsudon resists going fully soggy — you want both textures in one bite.

udon.name: The name likely descends from the Chinese konton/wonton (混飩) — boiled wheat dough — though by the Muromachi era ‘udon’ had drifted to mean the long, thick noodle we know today.
udon.history: Udon’s lineage runs back to wheat-noodle techniques imported from Tang China; legend credits the monk Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi) with carrying the method home to Sanuki (modern Kagawa) in the 9th century, though this tale is almost certainly later folklore rather than record. The noodle’s documented spread came in the Edo period, when pilgrims to Kotohira Shrine ate Sanuki udon at the gates and carried its fame across Japan, and udon shops multiplied in the cities.
udon.culture: Udon is everyday comfort and regional identity at once: Kagawa still calls itself ‘Udon Prefecture,’ self-serve Sanuki counters are a way of life, and the toppings carry their own lore — kitsune (sweet fried aburaage) named for the fox’s mythical fondness for it. In Kyoto and the Kansai world it leans gentle and refined, the broth meant to be drained to the bottom of the bowl.
udon.taste: The Kansai tell is the broth: pale gold and nearly clear, built on kombu and a whisper of katsuobushi with usukuchi (light soy), so it reads as aroma and clean salt rather than dark soy punch — taste it before the noodles and you should smell the sea-kelp sweetness. Then judge the koshi: a good noodle has a springy, slightly resistant bite (Sanuki’s squared strands chew firmest), a smooth slick surface, and enough body to stay distinct in the soft, light broth instead of dissolving into it.
udon.gf: Udon is wheat-flour noodles in a dashi that usually carries soy sauce and often a mirin or bonito base, so both noodle and broth are typical gluten traps — even ‘plain’ kake tsuyu and the sweet aburaage simmering liquid commonly contain wheat; ask.
udon.vegan: Most udon broth is built on katsuobushi (bonito) and sometimes iriko (sardine) dashi, and kitsune’s aburaage is often simmered in that same fish stock, so a kombu-only vegan version exists but must be requested, not assumed.

okonomiyaki.name: ’Okonomi’ (お好み) means ’what you like, as you like it’; ’yaki’ means grilled. The name is a promise of agency — you choose what goes in — and it began as exactly that: 1930s teppan tables where diners cooked their own preferred ingredients to taste.
okonomiyaki.history: Ancestry runs back through funoyaki, a thin sweet wheat-gluten crepe linked to tea-ceremony culture, and the Taishō-era issen-yōshoku (’one-sen Western food’) — a batter pancake with Worcester sauce and scallion sold cheap to children. The savory modern dish crystallized after WWII, when American aid flooded Japan with surplus wheat flour and cabbage, turning a griddle and a few yen into a meal; the word itself appears in print as early as a 1918 Yomiuri piece, though its current form is postwar.
okonomiyaki.culture: The great schism is structural, not seasoning. Kansai/Osaka style folds shredded cabbage, egg, and fillings into the batter and cooks the lot as one disk on the teppan; Hiroshima style is built in layers — a paper-thin crepe, a towering mound of cabbage steamed down, a nest of yakisoba noodles, then egg, assembled like architecture. Osaka argues volume and mix; Hiroshima argues engineering, and both cities will tell you, good-naturedly, that the other is doing it wrong.
okonomiyaki.taste: Judge it by the cabbage: a great one is mostly cabbage, barely bound, never overmixed into paste — you want it steamed sweet and tender inside with lacy, crisp browned edges where it met the iron. Listen for the layered crackle in Hiroshima style and the give of the noodles; in either, the dark Worcester-style sauce, snowfall of aonori, and katsuobushi flakes (curling alive in the rising heat) should sit on top, the mayo drawn in thin lines, not drowning the thing.
okonomiyaki.gf: The batter, the brushed sauce, the yakisoba noodles, and often the dashi are all wheat-based, so standard okonomiyaki is wheat at its core, not a stray topping — ask the shop directly about every component.
okonomiyaki.vegan: Dashi, egg in the batter, katsuobushi, pork, and egg-based mayo make the classic preparation thoroughly non-vegan; a true plant version needs a from-scratch rebuild.

yakitori.name: The name is plain to the point of disguise — yaki (grilled) plus tori (bird) — but for most of its history those skewers were not chicken at all. Under Japan’s long Buddhist-rooted taboo on four-legged meat, ‘tori’ honestly meant wild fowl: sparrow, pheasant, quail, anything the net could take. The word kept its modesty even after broiler chicken made it literal.
yakitori.history: The taboo runs deep: Emperor Tenmu’s edict of 675 forbade eating cattle, horses, dogs, monkeys and domestic fowl, and successive prohibitions held in some form until Emperor Meiji formally lifted them in 1872. Edo-period vendors worked the loophole, charring skewered wild birds and, as demand outran supply, offal and odd cuts. Chicken itself stayed a luxury until the postwar years, when American-introduced broiler farming collapsed the price and turned yakitori from a treat into the everyday smoke of the izakaya street.
yakitori.culture: Yakitori is the patron saint of the after-work hour — the red lantern, the cramped counter, the first cold beer landing as the grillmaster fans his coals. Its ethos is nose-to-tail thrift dressed as connoisseurship: momo (thigh) and negima (thigh-and-leek) for the crowd, but also tsukune (meatball), kawa (skin), bonjiri (tail), hatsu (heart), sunagimo (gizzard), reba (liver), nankotsu (cartilage) — a single bird parceled across a dozen sticks so nothing is wasted and everything has its devotee.
yakitori.taste: Read the char first: you want lacquered edges and a whisper of binchōtan smoke, never sooty bitterness or a pallid steamed look. On a shio skewer the salt should sharpen the chicken’s own fat; on a tare skewer the glaze should be glossy and layered, the mark of a master pot replenished daily for years. Press for the cut’s signature — kawa crisp-then-yielding, bonjiri almost molten with fat, tsukune bouncy against the raw-yolk dip — and check momo and reba for that just-blushing center that means juicy, not dry.
yakitori.gf: The classic trap is the tare glaze, built on wheat-based soy sauce, so the safer route is ordering skewers shio (salt only) — but ask whether the shio brush, grill, or seasonings share surfaces with tare before you trust it.
yakitori.vegan: This is grilled chicken — skin, organs, fat and all — so it is not vegan.

unagi.name: うなぎ (unagi) names the freshwater eel itself; kabayaki — the split, skewered, tare-lacquered grill — likely takes its name from the early skewered-whole shape said to resemble a cattail spike (kaba), though the etymology is debated.
unagi.history: Eel has been eaten in Japan since the Jōmon period, but kabayaki as we know it crystallized in Edo, refined through the Genroku era and after. Its midsummer fame is bound to Doyō-no-Ushi-no-Hi, the eel-eating day popularly credited to the Edo polymath Hiraga Gennai, who supposedly told a struggling eel-seller to hang a sign reading ‘Today, Day of the Ox’ — a tidy marketing fable whose source is unattested, though the custom did spread in the An’ei–Tenmei years (1772–1789), squarely within Gennai’s lifetime (he died 1779).
unagi.culture: The defining divide is Kantō versus Kansai. Tokyo splits the eel along the back — said to avoid the belly-cut that evoked samurai seppuku — then steams before grilling for a soft, melting fluff; Osaka, the merchant city untroubled by such symbolism, splits the belly and grills straight over coals for crisp edges and rendered fat. Served as unadon in a donburi or, more lavishly, unaju in a lacquered jūbako, often with kimosui and a shake of sanshō.
unagi.taste: Hunt for the contrast: skin lacquered crisp and faintly charred over flesh that should be silky, almost custardy with fat — fluffier in Kantō steam-grills, more bronzed and chewy-edged in Kansai. The tare should gloss and season, never drown; you want the eel’s own sweetness, not just caramel. Dust on sanshō and let its citrus-pepper tingle lift the richness off your palate.
unagi.gf: The kabayaki tare is built on wheat soy sauce and usually mirin, so it is not gluten-free as served — ask the shop about their tare and whether tamari is possible.
unagi.vegan: It is grilled eel — an animal — so unagi is not vegan.

onigiri.name: Onigiri (おにぎり) draws on nigiru, ‘to grasp, to press’; the gentler synonym omusubi nods to the creation kami Takamimusubi and Kamimusubi, the mountain shape a way of borrowing divine power. The two words are largely interchangeable, though omusubi keeps the older, more reverent ring.
onigiri.history: The lineage is almost absurdly deep: carbonized lumps of hand-pressed rice dated to the Yayoi era (roughly two millennia ago) were unearthed in Ishikawa, finger-marks still legible. By the Heian period the court packed tonjiki, small molded rice forms, for outdoor banquets, while the Hitachi gazetteer of 721 already records nigiri-ihi. Nori wrapping is comparatively modern, spreading only with Edo-period (Genroku, 1688–1704) seaweed-sheet farming.
onigiri.culture: Salt and the sour-salty umeboshi were the technology that made onigiri matter: a rice ball that traveled, that fed soldiers, pilgrims, and rice-planters through a day in the field without spoiling. The triangle is often romanticized as a mountain offered to the gods, though everyday shapes were round and barrel-like too; the konbini, with its ingenious film that keeps nori crisp and apart from the rice until you tear it, turned this ancient ration into a national icon.
onigiri.taste: Want rice that is gently gathered, not packed into a brick: it should hold its shape yet collapse into distinct, body-temperature grains, faintly glossy, seasoned so the salt sings just at the edge of the bite. The filling should sit as a generous core rather than a mean smear, and the nori is its own decision, crisp and shattering when freshly wrapped, pliant and toasty-sweet once it has softened into the rice.
onigiri.gf: The rice and nori are usually fine, but the filling is the risk: salmon, konbu tsukudani, mentaiko, tuna-mayo and seasoned rice mixes often carry soy sauce, dashi, or wheat, while plain umeboshi or salt is your safest bet, so ask.
onigiri.vegan: Plain salt, umeboshi, or pure konbu can be vegan, but tuna-mayo, salmon, bonito-flecked fillings, and many konbu tsukudani made with katsuo are not, so ask.

oden.name: The name hides a small etymological pilgrimage: oden descends from dengaku (田楽), skewered tofu glazed with miso and grilled, said to be named for the dengaku-mai harvest dancers who balanced on a single stilt — just as a block of white tofu balances on its bamboo skewer. Court ladies attached the honorific お (o) to den, giving o-den; when cooks later abandoned the miso glaze and simmered the skewers in dashi, the name stayed and the dish transformed beneath it.
oden.history: Miso dengaku flourished from the Muromachi period, and by the Edo era a simmered offshoot — ingredients stewed in soy-seasoned broth rather than painted with miso — had taken hold, eventually swapping miso entirely for dashi. The dish modern Japanese know coalesced in the 19th and 20th centuries, and in Kansai it is still affectionately called Kantō-daki (関東炊き), a name preserving the memory that the simmered style traveled west from Tokyo.
oden.culture: Oden is winter incarnate — the steam off a partitioned pot is shorthand for cold nights, and since the late 20th century the konbini counter has made it a ritual of December, ladled into cups beside the register. You order it item by item, pointing into the simmering grid, and the regional broth divide is real: Kantō leans on katsuobushi and dark koikuchi soy for a deeper, browner liquid, while Kyoto and Kansai favor a paler, kombu-forward dashi with usukuchi soy that lets each ingredient show its own color.
oden.taste: Hunt first for the daikon — translucent, amber-stained at the edges, collapsing at the chopstick after hours of drinking the dashi until broth and radish are the same thing. Read the broth itself: katsuobushi savor and a saline-sweet undertow, lighter and more translucent in a Kyoto pot. Then taste for contrast in texture — the rubbery snap of konnyaku, the spongy soak of atsuage, the springy fish pastes — and the sharp clarifying sinus-hit of karashi mustard dabbed on the rim.
oden.gf: Wheat hides in several oden classics — many fish-paste items (chikuwa, hanpen, satsuma-age) use wheat starch or binder, and the simmering broth is soy-seasoned dashi, so the liquid itself is rarely gluten-free; ask about both the specific item and the soy in the broth.
oden.vegan: The broth is bonito dashi, so even plant items like daikon, konnyaku, and tofu arrive steeped in fish stock; ask whether a kombu-only broth is possible and confirm each item, since some fish pastes look deceptively vegetable.

yuba.history: Yuba arrived in Japan in the embrace of Zen Buddhism, most likely carried over from Tang China by returning monks; a fond Kyoto legend credits Saichō, founder of Mt. Hiei’s Enryaku-ji, though the precise date is lost to record. By the Kamakura period (1185–1333) it was being produced at the Zen temples ringing the old capital, a quiet protein for monks bound to a meatless table. From those cloisters it spread to the temple towns of Nikkō and Minobu before stepping out into Edo-era kitchens, but Kyoto remained its spiritual home.
yuba.culture: The name 湯葉 reads literally as ‘hot-water leaf,’ for the thin, leaf-like film that wrinkles across the surface of simmering soy milk and is lifted away by hand. Kyoto’s soft, mineral-clean water and its deep bench of tofu artisans made the city the natural capital of the craft, and yuba became a pillar of both shōjin (temple) cooking and the refined theater of kaiseki. Connoisseurs distinguish silky fresh nama-yuba, eaten sashimi-like within hours, from the dried hiki-age and sheet yuba developed for keeping before refrigeration; Kyoto pulls its film in a single layer (湯葉), where Nikkō folds it double and writes it 湯波.
yuba.taste: Taste it plain and you meet the soybean undisguised: a clean, faintly sweet concentration of bean with no bitterness, because nothing has been added but heat and time. The pleasure is as much in the mouth as on the palate; nama-yuba is tender and slippery, almost custard-soft, with a creamy richness that coats the tongue, while the first sheet lifted from the pot is prized as the finest of all. Eat it the Kyoto way, with a dab of wasabi and a whisper of soy, or floating in a light dashi, so the bean leads and you can read the freshness and the cleanness of the lift.
yuba.gf: Yuba itself is nothing but soybean and water, so the leaf is naturally gluten-free, but the wasabi-soy, ponzu, and simmering broths it travels with routinely carry wheat-soy sauce or wheat-thickened dashi, so confirm the sauce and broth before you commit, and always ask.
yuba.vegan: Pure pressed soy milk, yuba is wholly plant-based and one of Japan’s most prized vegetable proteins, a staple of shōjin temple cooking; the only thing to watch is that some restaurant preparations poach or dress it in a bonito (katsuo) dashi, so ask whether the broth is kombu-only.

obanzai_moriawase.name: Written お番菜 — the 番 is the “ordinary, everyday” of bancha (common tea) and bangasa (a plain umbrella). So obanzai means simply “everyday side dishes,” the daily okazu of a Kyoto household. The irony: real Kyotoites rarely use the word — they just say “okazu.” It spread as faux-Kyoto-dialect after a 1964 Asahi Shimbun Kyoto column ran under the title “Obanzai.”
obanzai_moriawase.history: The idea long predates the fashionable name: an 1849 cookbook, Nenjū-Banzairoku, already collected 119 everyday home dishes. What makes Kyoto’s version distinct is the ethic behind it — shimatsu (始末), a merchant-household discipline of thrift that uses every ingredient fully and wastes nothing — and the city’s calendar of machishū (townsfolk) cooking, tuned to the seasons and annual observances.
obanzai_moriawase.culture: Obanzai lives by hare and ke — the strict old split between special “hare” days (festivals, when a household might make saba-zushi or order lavish shidashi catering) and ordinary “ke” days, when you ate obanzai. It is home food made from Kyoto’s own vegetables (kyō-yasai), simmered, quiet, and seasonal — the antithesis of kaiseki’s ceremony, and arguably the truer taste of how Kyoto actually eats.
obanzai_moriawase.taste: Taste for gentleness and the dashi. These dishes are seasoned lightly with Kyoto’s pale usukuchi soy so the vegetable, not the sauce, leads — simmered greens with abura-age, kabocha, hijiki, kiriboshi daikon. Look for balance across the whole spread rather than a single star, for the way leftovers are honoured rather than hidden, and for whatever vegetable is at its peak that week.
obanzai_moriawase.gf: Vegetable-forward but soy- and dashi-based; usukuchi soy is wheat and the dashi is often bonito — ask dish by dish.
obanzai_moriawase.vegan: Often vegetable-based, but the simmering dashi is typically bonito (and a few dishes use dried fish) — confirm each; kombu-dashi versions exist.

shojin_ryori_course.name: 精進 (shōjin) renders the Sanskrit vīrya — diligent devotion on the Buddhist path. Shōjin-ryōri is the cooking of that devotion: food prepared and eaten as a form of practice, not merely as sustenance.
shojin_ryori_course.history: Its rules were set in 13th-century Japan by Dōgen, founder of Sōtō Zen, who returned from China and wrote the Tenzo Kyōkun (“Instructions for the Cook”) and Fushukuhanpō (guidelines for eating). Dōgen treated the temple kitchen as a hall of awakening and the tenzo — the monk-cook — as one of its highest offices. From the monastery, shōjin’s aesthetics flowed outward and helped shape kaiseki and washoku at large.
shojin_ryori_course.culture: Rooted in the precept of non-killing (fusesshō), it uses no meat or fish — and omits the five pungent roots (gokun: garlic, onion and their kin) believed to inflame the passions. It is built on the temple grammar of five colours, five flavours and five cooking methods, crowned by tanmi, the prized “plain” taste. In Kyoto you meet it at Zen temples and specialists; Mt Kōya’s temple lodgings (shukubō) are its most famous home, and goma-dōfu — sesame “tofu” — its signature.
shojin_ryori_course.taste: Taste for what’s achieved without meat, fish, or even garlic: depth coaxed from kombu and shiitake, richness from sesame and fried tofu, and the clean tanmi meant to be the height of flavour rather than its absence. Look for goma-dōfu’s springy, nutty body, the seasonal mountain vegetables (sansai), and the quiet completeness of a meal built to leave you nourished and calm, never heavy.
shojin_ryori_course.gf: Plant-based but not automatically GF: it leans on soy sauce and miso, and wheat gluten itself (fu, and seitan-like mock meats) is a temple-cuisine tradition — ask for tamari and check for fu.
shojin_ryori_course.vegan: Vegan by definition — designed plant-based centuries before the word existed. At non-temple restaurants, just confirm a kombu/shiitake (not bonito) dashi.

tofu.what: Tofu is soybeans turned to silk: dried beans soaked, ground, and boiled into soy milk, then curdled — most traditionally with nigari, the magnesium-chloride brine left after sea salt is drawn from seawater — so the proteins set into a tender, pressable curd. Momen (’cotton’) tofu is curdled, broken, and pressed under cloth into a firmer, faintly textured block; kinugoshi (’silk-strained’) tofu is set whole in its container so nothing is pressed out, leaving it custard-soft and glassy.
tofu.history: Tofu is generally held to have entered Japan around the Nara–Heian eras with monks returning from Tang China, arriving as part of Buddhist temple culture rather than as a peasant food; the often-told tale crediting the Han prince Liu An with its invention is a much later, likely apocryphal embellishment. Sustained by shōjin ryōri — the strictly vegetarian cooking of Zen temples, where the precept against killing made the soybean a vital protein — tofu spread from monastery kitchens into the wider Japanese table over the centuries.
tofu.culture: Kyoto is Japan’s celebrated tofu city, and the reason is in the ground: the basin’s soft, low-mineral spring water yields an especially delicate, clean-tasting curd, and old shops have built reputations on water as much as beans. Around the temples — most famously the precincts of Nanzen-ji — yudōfu, blocks of tofu warmed gently in kombu broth, became a quietly devotional way to eat, the dish a temple-cuisine inheritance turned local pride. And tofu is only the most visible face of the soybean, the secret engine of washoku that also yields shōyu, miso, yuba, edamame, kinako, abura-age, and natto.
tofu.taste: Fine tofu tastes of almost nothing and everything at once: a clean, faintly sweet soybean flavor with a fresh, milky fragrance, carried on a texture that is either silky and trembling (kinugoshi) or tender and lightly grained (momen). The whole point is restraint — eaten cold as hiyayakko with a little ginger and shōyu, or warm as yudōfu lifted straight from the broth — so that the quality of the bean and, in Kyoto especially, the softness of the water are what you actually taste. Drown it in sauce and you have erased it; the pleasure is in how little it needs.
tofu.gf: Tofu itself is gluten-free, but its companions often are not — soy sauce and ponzu usually contain wheat, and agedashi tofu’s coating and broth typically do too, so ask.
tofu.vegan: Tofu is fully plant-based and a cornerstone vegan protein; just be wary of the dashi-based sauces and simmering broths (often bonito-laced) that are commonly served alongside it.

nasu_dengaku.history: Dengaku began as skewered tofu grilled and brushed with miso in the medieval period, a name borrowed from the fields: dengaku-mai, the white-hakama folk dance of rice-planting rites. By the Bunka-Bunsei era (1804–1830), Edo and Kamigata cooks had broadened it to daikon, lotus root, taro and eggplant, and the vegetable versions became the everyday face of dengaku.
nasu_dengaku.culture: The name is the gem. A dengaku-mai performer danced balanced on a takaashi, a single tall bamboo stilt, in white robes; a pale block of tofu speared on its skewer was thought to mirror that one-legged dancer, and the food took the dance’s name. In Kyoto the glaze is classically a kinome (young sansho leaf) miso ground smooth — Edo-period records note Kamigata grinds the leaf into the miso while Edo merely lays it on top — and the dish reaches its peak with summer’s broad, dense Kamo nasu.
nasu_dengaku.taste: Look for eggplant cooked until the flesh collapses to something custardy and silken, drinking up oil and a faint char while the skin still holds the shape like a cup. Over it sits the dengaku miso as a glossy lacquer, sweet-savory and almost caramelized, often crowned with toasted sesame or a bruised kinome leaf whose green-citrus snap cuts the richness; the whole pleasure is that dark sweet miso playing against the mild, creamy near-nothingness of the eggplant, eaten hot.
nasu_dengaku.gf: The glaze can hide gluten — many miso are brewed with barley or wheat (mugi miso), and dengaku miso is usually loosened with mirin and sake, so even rice-based saikyo versions vary by kitchen; ask.
nasu_dengaku.vegan: Eggplant plus sweet miso is plant-based at heart, but dengaku miso is sometimes enriched with dashi or egg yolk (especially the kimi/tamago style), so it is not vegan by default; ask.

warabimochi.history: A bracken-starch jelly enjoyed since the Heian court, associated with Kansai summers.
warabimochi.culture: Soft, cool, translucent pieces dusted with kinako (roasted soy flour) and drizzled with kuromitsu syrup — a refreshing wagashi.
warabimochi.gf: True warabi-starch versions are GF, but many use wheat starch — verify.
warabimochi.vegan: Plant-based (starch, soy flour, sugar syrup) — vegan.

shoyu_ramen.history: Tokyo-style ramen in its original form — Asakusa's Rairaiken (1910) served Chinese-style wheat noodles in a clear chicken-and-dashi broth seasoned with a soy (shōyu) tare, and this restrained, soy-led bowl became the template that every later regional style departed from.
shoyu_ramen.culture: The most classic ramen profile: a clear, light-brown, soy-forward broth that stays noodle-forward rather than rich. Toppings are traditionally modest — chāshū, menma, nori, a soft egg, scallion. Regulars judge a shop on the balance of tare and broth, not on heaviness.
shoyu_ramen.gf: Wheat noodles, wheat soy tare, and pork/fish broth — not GF.
shoyu_ramen.vegan: Chicken/fish broth and soy tare; rarely vegan.

miso_ramen.history: Born in 1950s Sapporo (Aji no Sanpei), a hearty miso-tare ramen built for Hokkaido winters.
miso_ramen.culture: Rich and sweet-savory, often with corn, butter, and ground pork — the northern icon.
miso_ramen.gf: Wheat noodles; miso tare may contain barley/wheat; pork broth — not GF.
miso_ramen.vegan: Usually pork broth and ground pork; vegan miso ramen exists but is the exception.

chuka_soba.history: An older name for soy-based ramen — literally "Chinese noodles" — evoking the Showa shokudo era.
chuka_soba.culture: Nostalgic, simple ramen; the retro term signals a classic style.
chuka_soba.gf: Wheat noodles, soy tare, pork/fish broth — not GF.

hiyashi_chuka.history: Cold ramen with toppings and a tangy sauce — a Showa-era summer invention.
hiyashi_chuka.culture: Summer-only at many shops, with colorful julienned toppings.
hiyashi_chuka.gf: Wheat noodles + soy/vinegar sauce (wheat) — not GF.
hiyashi_chuka.vegan: Usually has ham, egg, and sometimes dashi — not vegan as standard.

champon.name: Champon (ちゃんぽん) — Nagasaki’s great all-in-one bowl, born where the Min coast of Fujian met the harbor of a treaty port. Founder Chin Heijun originally listed it as ‘shina udon’ (Chinese udon) before the city came to call it champon.
champon.history: It was created at the turn of the twentieth century at Shikairō (四海樓, ‘House of the Four Seas’), the Nagasaki Chinese restaurant Chin Heijun (Chen Pingshun) founded in 1899 (Meiji 32) after emigrating from Fuzhou in Fujian. He devised it as a cheap, hearty, nutritious bowl for the poor Chinese students and overseas Chinese of the port, adapting the Fujianese dish tāngròusīmiàn into something that filled a struggling diner in one go.
champon.culture: More than a noodle dish, champon is Nagasaki’s edible self-portrait — a Sino-Japanese hybrid of a city that stayed open to the world through centuries of seclusion, now its signature alongside the related sara-udon. The very name carries that mixed lineage: most trace it to the Fujian Min greeting ‘chia̋h-pn̄g’ (‘have you eaten?’), while others hear an onomatopoeia for the clatter of mixing or the chan-pon of bell and drum — a debate as layered as the bowl. Chains like Ringer Hut have since carried it nationwide, but the original simmers on at Shikairō overlooking the bay.
champon.taste: Look first for the broth: a milky, ivory pork-and-chicken soup, rich but not heavy, lifted by a clean oceanic sweetness leached from the squid, shrimp and kamaboko. Unlike ramen, the thick, springy noodles — made with Nagasaki’s distinctive ‘tōaku’ lye water — are simmered right in the soup, so they drink it up and arrive plump and slick, faintly chewy at the core. Then comes the abundance: a generous tangle of lard-fried cabbage and bean sprouts, pink-and-white kamaboko, slippery kikurage, squid and shrimp, every spoonful a different texture and a different sweetness against that pork-bone base.
champon.gf: Not gluten-free — the thick champon noodles are wheat, and the pork-chicken broth, kamaboko, and seasonings typically carry soy and other wheat-based ingredients; ask the shop about every component.
champon.vegan: Not vegan — the foundation is a pork-and-chicken bone broth loaded with seafood (squid, shrimp, kamaboko) and lard-fried toppings.

kake.history: "Kake" is noodles served simply in hot dashi broth — the most basic preparation of udon or soba.
kake.culture: The everyday standby: cheap, fast, warming, and the canvas for toppings.
kake.gf: Broth is dashi-soy (wheat + fish); udon is wheat and soba is usually wheat-cut — not GF.
kake.vegan: Bonito dashi; ask for kombu. Noodles themselves are often vegan.

kake_soba.name: Soba (そば) names the buckwheat grain itself before it ever named a noodle; the cut noodle is properly soba-kiri (蕎麦切り), ‘sliced buckwheat,’ a term first glimpsed in a 1574 ledger at Jōshō-ji temple in Kiso recording soba-kiri served to carpenters mending the hall.
kake_soba.history: Buckwheat is a survivor’s crop — it ripens fast in cold, thin mountain soil where rice fails, and for centuries it was famine insurance ground into porridge and dumplings (sobagaki). Only in the Edo period did the grain become a noodle in earnest; early soba-kiri, bound with tofu or rice broth and prone to snapping, gave way to the wheat-bound version that let Edo’s street stalls spin out the long, clean strands we know.
kake_soba.culture: Edo made soba a civic ritual: slurped loudly (the draw of air carries the aroma), the cold noodle barely kissed into the corner of the tsuyu rather than drowned, and the meal closed by pouring sobayu — the starchy, vitamin-rich boiling water, a Shinshu custom — into the leftover dip to drink it down. Custom clings to the calendar too: toshikoshi-soba on New Year’s Eve, the long strands wishing long life while the easily-bitten noodle severs the old year’s misfortune.
kake_soba.taste: Lift a strand before the tsuyu and breathe — fresh-milled soba smells faintly of roasted grain and earth; stale flour smells of nothing. The noodle should have a defined edge and a brief springy snap rather than udon’s soft yield, the speckled gray-brown flecks (hokkō) marking real hull in the grind. Judge the tsuyu for balance — dashi, soy and mirin in tension, never just salty — then thin the dregs with sobayu and drink: that cloudy cup is where the rutin and the truest buckwheat flavor settle.
kake_soba.gf: Pure ten-wari (juwari) soba is 100% buckwheat and gluten-free, but most shops serve nihachi — 二八, 80% buckwheat to 20% wheat for binding — and the tsuyu is built on wheat-bearing soy sauce, so cross-contamination at a shared boil is routine; never assume, always ask.
kake_soba.vegan: The noodle is plant-based, but the kake broth and dipping tsuyu are almost always built on katsuobushi (bonito) dashi, so even a plain bowl carries fish — ask for a kombu-only base or skip it.

zaru.history: Chilled noodles on a bamboo zaru with dipping tsuyu — Edo summer eating.
zaru.culture: Refreshing and minimalist; dip, slurp, then drink the cooking water (sobayu).
zaru.gf: Tsuyu is soy + dashi (wheat + fish); soba is usually wheat-cut, udon is wheat — not GF.
zaru.vegan: Tsuyu uses bonito dashi — vegan only if kombu-based.

zaru_soba.history: The definitive cold soba on a bamboo screen, topped with nori — Edo's summer noodle.
zaru_soba.culture: Purists judge a shop by it: dip lightly and slurp.
zaru_soba.gf: Most soba is cut with wheat (nihachi); only juwari (100% buckwheat) is GF — and the tsuyu still has wheat soy + fish.
zaru_soba.vegan: Tsuyu is bonito-based; a kombu tsuyu makes it vegan.

oroshi_soba.history: Cold soba with grated daikon (oroshi) — a Fukui specialty that cuts the richness.
oroshi_soba.culture: Sharp and refreshing; the daikon adds bite.
oroshi_soba.gf: Soba usually wheat-cut + soy-dashi tsuyu — not GF unless juwari + GF tsuyu.
oroshi_soba.vegan: Bonito tsuyu; a kombu version is vegan.

tororo_soba.history: Soba topped with grated yam (tororo) — a slippery, nourishing classic.
tororo_soba.culture: Prized as a stamina dish for its silky texture.
tororo_soba.gf: Soba is usually wheat-cut + soy-dashi tsuyu — not GF unless juwari; the yam itself is GF.
tororo_soba.vegan: Bonito tsuyu; the yam is vegan — ask for kombu tsuyu.

kamo_seiro.history: Cold soba dipped in a hot duck-and-leek broth — a refined Edo combination.
kamo_seiro.culture: A connoisseur's favorite, especially in cold months.
kamo_seiro.gf: Soba usually wheat-cut + soy-dashi duck broth — not GF.

sobagaki.history: Sobagaki is buckwheat in its elder form, eaten centuries before anyone thought to cut it into noodles. Its roots reach back toward the Nara and Heian eras, when buckwheat was a hardy hedge against rice failure, and it spread once Kamakura-period stone mills made flour easy to grind. Until the mid-Edo period, ‘soba’ meant this stirred mass, not the soba-kiri noodles that eventually eclipsed it — in some farming villages noodle-cutting was even dismissed as too refined for field hands, so the old dumpling lived on.
sobagaki.culture: The name tells the method: gaki comes from kaku (掻く), to stir or whip, for the brisk beating of buckwheat flour and hot water into a single soft mass. Born as mountain fare and famine food, it survives today as the connoisseur’s dish — what serious Kyoto soba houses set before a guest who wants to taste the flour itself, not the craft of the cut. It is eaten plainly with soy and sharp wasabi, afloat in dashi, or in older country fashion sweetened with sugar.
sobagaki.taste: Because there is no wheat to soften it and no boiling water to leach it, sobagaki is the purest reading of buckwheat you can order — so judge it on nuttiness and that fleeting, almost floral grain fragrance that rises strongest the instant it is made. The texture should be warm, soft and freshly stirred, faintly springy with a gentle mochi-like cling, never gummy or fully set; a good one carries a clean, earthy sweetness on the finish. Eat it at once: let it sit and the aroma fades while the body stiffens.
sobagaki.gf: Pure sobagaki is buckwheat and water, which can be gluten-free, but it is usually served with a wheat-soy dipping sauce or bonito dashi, and shops that also cut wheat-blend soba carry real cross-contact risk — ask before ordering.
sobagaki.vegan: The dumpling itself is plant-based, but the tsuyu or dashi it comes with is almost always made with bonito, so confirm a kombu-only broth (or take it with plain soy) and ask.

kitsune.history: Credited to late-19th-century Osaka, where the sweet-simmered age over udon caught on and spread across Kansai before going national. In Kyoto, remember it sits one step from 'tanuki' — same age, different treatment of the broth.
kitsune.culture: Pure comfort food, and a dish wrapped in mythology: to eat kitsune is to eat the fox's offering. Inexpensive, warming, ordered without a second thought by everyone from schoolkids to grandmothers.
kitsune.gf: Wheat udon, soy, dashi — not GF.
kitsune.vegan: Age is plant-based, but the dashi is usually bonito — ask.
kitsune.name: 'Fox.' Udon (or soba) topped with a sheet of sweet-simmered abura-age. Why fox? Because abura-age is the fox's favourite food in folklore — and the fox is the messenger of Inari, the rice god. The same belief names inari-zushi, so kitsune and inari are siblings, both pointing back to the fox's love of fried tofu.
kitsune.taste: The age is everything: it should be plump, golden, and so soaked with sweet dashi that it floods the mouth when bitten. The udon soft and slippery, the broth light enough to let the tofu's sweetness read against it rather than fight it.

kitsune_udon.history: Udon topped with sweet fried tofu (aburaage) — the Kansai comfort bowl par excellence (Osaka origin).
kitsune_udon.culture: Gentle and sweet-savory; the everyday favorite.
kitsune_udon.gf: Wheat udon + soy-dashi broth — not GF.
kitsune_udon.vegan: Aburaage is plant-based; the broth is fish dashi — ask for kombu.

tanuki.name: “Tanuki” is the raccoon-dog — folklore’s shape-shifting trickster, not the “badger” of loose translation. The word names different dishes in different cities, and even the reasons differ. In Tokyo, tanuki is soba or udon strewn with tenkasu (tempura crumbs); the leading theory derives the name from tane-nuki (種抜き), “filling removed” — the batter with its shrimp taken out. Kyoto’s tanuki is a wholly different dish with its own story: it is kitsune udon (sweet fried tofu) cloaked in a thick kuzu-arrowroot あん, and Kyotoites explain it with a wink — 「きつねがドロンと化けてたぬきになった」, “the fox went *doron!* and shape-shifted into a tanuki.” The trickster reveals itself in the transforming sauce.
tanuki.history: Kyoto’s version is a response to the city’s notorious soko-bie — the winter cold that seems to rise straight out of the ground in the basin. Thickening the dashi with kuzu (arrowroot) traps the heat so the bowl stays scalding to the last mouthful, and grated ginger warms from within; plenty of Kyotoites still order ankake udon specifically when fighting off a cold, for its warmth and easy digestion. The cut is courtly, too: the abura-age is sliced into fine tanzaku strips — said to be so that even a maiko could eat it elegantly, without biting into a flapping sheet of tofu.
tanuki.culture: Order “tanuki” in Kyoto and you receive something a Tokyoite wouldn’t recognise — the clearest single lesson in how fiercely local this cuisine is. It is everyday, affectionate food, dressed with Kyoto’s own slender Kujō-negi; the beloved old standby Tonoda Shokudō, near Kyoto Station, is a quiet pilgrimage for a bowl of it.
tanuki.taste: Look for the kuzu-an clinging to every strand rather than pooling beneath — that glossy cloak is the whole point, and it should keep the bowl almost too hot to begin. The thin-sliced age should go soft and bleed its sweet broth into the sauce; the grated ginger should cut the starchy richness with a clean, warming sharpness. Stir up from the bottom, eat fast while it steams, and taste the Kujō-negi’s gentle sweetness playing against the ginger’s bite.
tanuki.gf: Wheat udon and a soy-seasoned dashi (the kuzu thickener itself is GF, but the broth is not) — not GF.
tanuki.vegan: The fried tofu is plant-based, but the dashi is typically bonito — ask for a kombu-only base.

niku_udon.history: Udon topped with sweet-simmered beef (or pork) — hearty everyday fare.
niku_udon.culture: Filling and warming; popular in western Japan.
niku_udon.gf: Wheat udon + soy-dashi broth + soy-simmered meat — not GF.
niku_udon.vegan: Meat and fish dashi — not vegan.

tempura_udon.what: Udon in hot dashi crowned with tempura (classically a prawn), the crust slowly softening into the broth.
tempura_udon.gf: Wheat noodles, wheat batter and a soy-dashi broth — not GF; ask.
tempura_udon.vegan: Prawn tempura and bonito dashi — ask.

nabeyaki_udon.history: Udon simmered in an individual clay pot with tempura, egg, and vegetables — a winter classic.
nabeyaki_udon.culture: Served bubbling in its donabe; a meal in a pot.
nabeyaki_udon.gf: Wheat udon + tempura + soy-dashi broth — not GF.
nabeyaki_udon.vegan: Egg, fish dashi, often shrimp tempura — not vegan.

bukkake.history: Noodles with a little concentrated tsuyu poured directly over (not for dipping) — a Sanuki/Okayama style.
bukkake.culture: Bold, direct flavor, topped with daikon, scallion, and tempura bits.
bukkake.gf: Wheat udon + soy-dashi tsuyu — not GF.
bukkake.vegan: Bonito tsuyu and varied toppings — ask for kombu tsuyu.

ebi_tempura.what: The star of the tempura world — a long prawn in a pale, lacy crust, skewered so it fries dead straight; sweet and snapping inside.
ebi_tempura.gf: Wheat batter and wheat-soy tentsuyu, fried in shared oil — not GF; ask.

tendon.what: Tempura over a bowl of rice, the fritters glossed with a sweet soy-mirin tare that soaks into the grains below.
tendon.gf: Wheat batter plus a wheat-soy tare — not GF; ask.
tendon.vegan: Usually seafood tempura with a fish-based tare — ask.

tempura_moriawase.what: An assorted tempura platter — seafood and seasonal vegetables fried to order, each piece timed to its own moment.
tempura_moriawase.gf: Wheat batter, tentsuyu and shared fry oil — not GF; ask.
tempura_moriawase.vegan: Vegetable pieces might qualify, but the batter (egg), shared oil and fish dashi usually rule it out — ask.

tempura_set.what: A teishoku built around tempura — the fritters with rice, miso soup and pickles.
tempura_set.gf: Battered and fried in wheat; the sauce and soup add more risk — not GF; ask.
tempura_set.vegan: Usually includes seafood, egg or fish dashi — ask.

tonkatsu_teishoku.what: The full tonkatsu set — the cutlet with shredded cabbage, rice, miso soup and pickles, refills included.
tonkatsu_teishoku.gf: Panko and sauce are wheat, fried in shared oil — not GF; ask.

katsu_sando.history: A breaded-cutlet sandwich on soft shokupan — a Showa-era yoshoku creation now turned gourmet.
katsu_sando.culture: Both a konbini staple and a fancy-café indulgence.
katsu_sando.gf: Wheat bread + panko cutlet + wheat sauce — not GF.
katsu_sando.vegan: Pork, egg, and often butter/mayo — not vegan.

kushikatsu.name: Kushikatsu (串カツ) — literally ‘skewered cutlet,’ and in its more refined registers called kushiage (串揚げ): bite-sized morsels of meat, seafood, and vegetables threaded on bamboo, jacketed in panko, and deep-fried.
kushikatsu.history: Born in Osaka’s Shinsekai district in the early Shōwa years — the shop Daruma dates itself to 1929 (Shōwa 4) — as cheap, filling fuel for the day laborers who crowded the area below Tsūtenkaku. The trick was economy: skewering scraps and offcuts (originally cheap beef) so a working man could eat fast, standing, for a few coins. Though now an Osaka emblem, some food historians trace an earlier breaded-skewer lineage to Tokyo, and the Shinsekai claim is best stated as the dish’s spiritual birthplace rather than its sole origin.
kushikatsu.culture: Kushikatsu enshrines the single most-recited rule in Japanese dining: 二度漬け禁止 — no double-dipping. Each shop sets out a communal stainless trough of thin sauce shared by every customer, so a bitten skewer never returns to it; if you want more, you ladle it on with the free shredded cabbage, which doubles as a hygienic scoop and a palate-cleanser. It is fundamentally counter food — elbows on a worn counter, beer in hand, skewers arriving and vanishing in quick succession, the opposite of Kyoto’s hushed kaiseki restraint.
kushikatsu.taste: The tell of a good one is the shell: a thin, blond, almost weightless panko crust that shatters rather than crunches, never oily, each skewer pulled at the exact second its filling peaks — beef still pink, renkon snapping, asparagus squeaking, a quail egg molten. Against that you taste the sauce: thin, dark, tangy and faintly sweet, a Worcester-style dashi-tinged liquid that soaks just the tip without sogging the crust. The raw cabbage between bites resets the palate, cutting the fry and resetting you for the next.
kushikatsu.gf: Thoroughly wheat: the coating is flour-and-panko breaded and deep-fried, and the communal dipping sauce is wheat-based Worcester-style — so always ask before assuming anything here is gluten-free.
kushikatsu.vegan: Mostly meat and seafood; vegetable skewers do exist but are battered and fried in shared oil, and the communal sauce can carry dashi — so ask.

gyudon.name: Don is the bowl; gyū is the beef — but the word itself is a Yoshinoya invention. The dish was born in the Meiji era as gyūmeshi (牛飯, ‘beef rice’), and founder Matsuda Eikichi is credited with coining ‘gyūdon’ around the turn of the century, rebranding cheap beef-over-rice into something with a name as quick as the meal.
gyudon.history: For over a millennium an imperial-and-Buddhist taboo kept beef off Japanese plates; the Meiji government lifted it in 1871, and the young emperor’s reported taste of beef the next year turned meat-eating into a patriotic act of modernization. The bowl descends from gyūnabe (the Tokyo beef hot-pot, traced to a Yokohama eatery around 1862) — civilization-and-enlightenment, bunmei-kaika, distilled into simmered beef. Yoshinoya, founded by Matsuda in 1899 in the Nihonbashi fish market, is widely credited as gyūdon’s birthplace, feeding fishmongers who had no time to sit.
gyudon.culture: Gyūdon is Japan’s patron saint of hayai-yasui-umai — fast, cheap, delicious — the meal of the worker, the student, the salaryman between trains. The chains (Yoshinoya, Sukiya, Matsuya) turned it into a national ritual of ordering by size and topping, eaten standing or in minutes at a counter. In Kyoto, where the slow temple-cuisine of shōjin-ryōri sets the register, the gyūdon counter is the unpretentious counterweight: honest fuel, no ceremony.
gyudon.taste: Judge it by the tsuyu — the soy-mirin-dashi should have soaked down into the rice so the bottom grains are stained and savory-sweet, not dry. Look for onions cooked to translucent collapse, surrendering their sugar into the broth, and beef sliced paper-thin so its fat half-melts rather than chews. Crown it with raw or onsen egg for silk, beni-shōga for a sharp pickled jolt, and shichimi for heat that cuts the richness.
gyudon.gf: The simmering tsuyu is built on soy sauce and mirin, so wheat is almost always present, and chain gyūdon is generally not gluten-free — ask before ordering.
gyudon.vegan: It is beef simmered in a dashi broth over rice — not vegan.

oyako_don.history: Born in late-19th-century Tokyo; the chicken restaurant Tamahide claims the original around 1891, simmering chicken and onion and binding it with barely-set egg over rice. It spread as fast, nourishing one-bowl food and never left the everyday table.
oyako_don.culture: One of the most ordinary and beloved donburi in Japan — lunch-counter staple, home-cook standby, the dish a child first learns to love. Its very plainness is why people are quietly fierce about how it should be done.
oyako_don.gf: The sauce is soy-based (wheat) — not GF unless made with tamari.
oyako_don.name: 'Parent-and-child bowl' — oya (parent) is the chicken, ko (child) is the egg, reunited over rice. It's a small, knowing joke. The beef-and-egg version, where the two aren't related, is wryly called tanin-don, the 'stranger's bowl.'
oyako_don.taste: The test is the egg: set to a just-trembling, half-runny custard, never dry or fully cooked. The chicken should be tender, the onion sweet and collapsing, and the dashi-soy-mirin sauce should have soaked only the top layer of rice. It should look glossy and barely hold its shape.

konoha_don.history: A "fallen-leaf" donburi of egg-bound kamaboko (fish cake) over rice — a thrifty regional bowl.
konoha_don.culture: A lighter cousin of katsudon and oyakodon.
konoha_don.gf: Egg + fish cake (wheat) + soy-dashi — not GF.
konoha_don.vegan: Egg and fish cake/dashi — not vegan.

tamago_toji.history: Ingredients bound with beaten egg in a dashi-soy broth ("egg-curtained") — the technique behind oyakodon and katsudon.
tamago_toji.culture: Soft and comforting; a home-cooking staple.

takikomi_gohan.history: Rice cooked together with seasonal ingredients in dashi and soy — a centuries-old way to stretch rice and mark the season.
takikomi_gohan.culture: Autumn's mushroom and chestnut versions are especially loved.
takikomi_gohan.gf: Seasoned with soy (wheat) and dashi (fish) — usually not GF.
takikomi_gohan.vegan: Often fish dashi and sometimes chicken; a vegetable + kombu version can be vegan.

ochazuke.history: The act of moistening rice with hot liquid is ancient — yuzuke (hot water over rice) and suihan appear in Heian classics like the Genji Monogatari and Makura no Sōshi, long predating any tea. Tea proper entered the bowl only when bancha and sencha became affordable to commoners in the middle Edo period; by the Genroku era, dedicated chazuke-ya were serving it as the city’s original fast food.
ochazuke.culture: Ochazuke is the dish of thrift and lateness — a way to honor leftover rice, the quick supper after drinking, the small kindness to oneself at the end of a day. In Kyoto it carries a sharper edge: the legendary bubu-zuke (bubu being the local word for tea), where a host’s offer of ochazuke to a lingering guest is the famous, dagger-soft hint that it is time to go — pure ikezu-bunka, Kyoto’s art of saying nothing and meaning everything. Treat that one as folklore polished by retelling, but it is culturally real.
ochazuke.taste: The pleasure is in the loosening: pour the hot tea or dashi and watch tight, warm grains slacken into something between rice and soup, the steam carrying roasted bancha or bonito. You want a sharp counterweight against the blandness — the puckering sourness of umeboshi, the cured salt of shake, a streak of wasabi — and a textural break from nori going soft or arare staying briefly crunchy before they surrender. Eat it fast, while it is hot and the contrasts are still alive.
ochazuke.gf: A rice-and-tea base can be simple, but commercial ochazuke mixes, the dashi, and arare or seasoning blends commonly carry soy and wheat, and toppings vary widely — ask before assuming.
ochazuke.vegan: Plain tea poured over rice can be vegan, but most versions lean on bonito- or fish-based dashi and toppings like salmon, mentaiko, or katsuobushi — ask how the broth is made and what is on top.

zosui.history: A rice soup simmered in dashi, often from leftover nabe broth — old, frugal, and restorative.
zosui.culture: The comforting finish to a hotpot, or a sickbed food.
zosui.gf: Dashi + soy (wheat + fish) — usually not GF.
zosui.vegan: Usually fish dashi and often egg/meat; a vegetable + kombu version can be vegan.

bento.history: Portable boxed meals dating back centuries; the ekiben (station bento) is a travel ritual.
bento.culture: A balanced, compartmentalized meal — both an art form and everyday convenience.
bento.gf: Mixed contents typically include soy-seasoned and fried (wheat) items — assume not GF without checking each.
bento.vegan: Usually contains fish, meat, or egg; vegan bento exist but are uncommon.

shojin_bento.what: A boxed form of temple cuisine — the same meat-free, five-principle cooking arranged for portability.
shojin_bento.gf: Plant-based but soy-heavy, and fu / mock-meat is wheat gluten — ask.
shojin_bento.vegan: Vegan by definition; confirm a kombu dashi.

shojin_ryori_set.what: A set of Buddhist temple cuisine — plant-based, seasonal dishes arranged as a balanced tray.
shojin_ryori_set.gf: Plant-based but soy- and miso-heavy, and fu (wheat gluten) is common — ask.
shojin_ryori_set.vegan: Vegan by definition; confirm a kombu (not bonito) dashi at non-temple restaurants.

kaiseki_course.name: Two different meals share the sound “kaiseki.” 懐石 — literally “stone in the bosom” — recalls the Zen practice of tucking a warmed stone (温石, onjaku) against the belly to quiet hunger during fasting; it names the spare meal served before tea, just enough to settle the stomach. 会席, also “kaiseki,” is its descendant: the banquet course built around enjoying sake. Most restaurant “kaiseki” today is the 会席 line; the tea meal is cha-kaiseki.
kaiseki_course.history: Cha-kaiseki took shape with the way of tea. As Sen no Rikyū (1522–1591) perfected wabi-cha in the Kyoto region, the meal before thick matcha was pared to its essence — ichijū-sansai, “one soup, three dishes,” restrained enough that nothing would overwhelm the tea. Over the centuries the looser, sake-forward 会席 grew out of it for banquets and poetry gatherings, and from there into the multi-course haute cuisine that now represents Japanese fine dining.
kaiseki_course.culture: Kaiseki is the apex of washoku, and Kyoto is its heartland. A meal follows a fixed procession — sakizuke, mukōzuke, wanmono, yakimono, the hassun tray — each course timed and plated to the exact season, often to the week. On the cha-kaiseki tray the placement itself carries meaning: rice to the left, soup to the right (in and yō, yin and yang), the mukōzuke set across. The governing spirit is ichigo-ichie, “one meeting, one chance” — this gathering will never recur.
kaiseki_course.taste: Don’t taste for richness — taste for restraint and shun, the season. Look for the dish that could only appear this fortnight: the first bamboo shoot, the year’s first matsutake. Notice how little gets in the ingredient’s way — a clear dashi, a whisper of pale usukuchi soy, one garnish that names the month. The pleasure is in noticing: the bowl chosen to match the food, the knife-work, the way flavours unfold from light to rich and home again to rice.
kaiseki_course.gf: Built on dashi and soy seasoning, with wheat soy sauce (and sometimes tempura or fu) across the courses — not inherently GF, but a serious kaiseki kitchen can often accommodate if told well in advance. Discuss when booking.
kaiseki_course.vegan: Traditional kaiseki centres seafood and a bonito dashi — not vegan by default. Its plant-based sibling, shōjin-style kaiseki, is the vegan path; arrange ahead.

omakase_course.what: 'I leave it to you' — a chef's-choice tasting, most often at a sushi counter, where the itamae sends out the day's best in sequence at his own pace.
omakase_course.gf: Depends entirely on the kitchen — tell the chef before you sit; soy, eel sauce, tamago and tempura are the usual wheat risks; arrange ahead and ask.
omakase_course.vegan: Seafood-centred by default; arrange far in advance.

nimono.history: Simmered dishes — vegetables, tofu, fish, or meat cooked slowly in dashi, soy, mirin, and sugar — a cornerstone of home and obanzai cooking.
nimono.culture: The quiet heart of washoku; seasonal and comforting.
nimono.gf: Simmering liquid uses soy (wheat) and dashi — usually not GF; ask.
nimono.vegan: Often fish dashi; a vegetable nimono with kombu dashi can be vegan — confirm.

takiawase.name: Takiawase (炊き合わせ) — literally ‘simmer’ (taki) and ‘to bring together’ (awase). The name is the whole philosophy: several ingredients each simmered apart, then composed into one bowl. In Kyoto’s kitchens you’ll also hear the dialect verb taita (炊いたん) for the same gentle simmering.
takiawase.history: A signature technique of Kansai cooking, refined in Kyoto across both formal kaiseki and the home-style obanzai table. It belongs to a culinary tradition built on water, kombu, and restraint rather than the darker, sweeter, soy-heavy nimono of eastern Japan, and it leans on the city’s prized kyo-yasai — heirloom vegetables grown for clean flavor and good color.
takiawase.culture: Takiawase is the opposite of a one-pot stew, and that distinction is the point of pride: where a stew lets everything blur, here each ingredient — taro, bamboo shoot, pumpkin, fu, tofu, sometimes fish — is cooked separately in its own seasoned dashi for its own ideal doneness, then arranged together so independence and harmony coexist in one vessel. It is profoundly seasonal, a quiet showcase of a cook’s judgment, and a hallmark of the pale usukuchi-soy style that seasons without staining.
takiawase.taste: Look for vegetables that hold their shape and keep their living color — kabocha still orange, greens still green, never gray and collapsed — each piece tasting cleanly and unmistakably of itself, the dashi tuned a little differently for every element rather than one flavor flattening the bowl. The pleasure is the contrast and the composition: a faintly sweet simmered taro beside a clean-bitter green, the broth gentle enough that you taste the vegetable first and the seasoning second. This is connoisseurship through subtraction — the mastery is in what the cook withheld.
takiawase.gf: Vegetable-forward but each element is simmered in a seasoned dashi that usually carries soy sauce (wheat) and often mirin, so it commonly contains gluten — ask how each item was seasoned.
takiawase.vegan: Vegetable-centered and sometimes built on kombu dashi, but the simmering stock is most often katsuo (bonito) dashi and a piece may include fish, so it is frequently not vegan — ask about the dashi for each component.

dashimaki_tamago.history: A rolled omelette bound with dashi, refined in Kyoto/Kansai where it's softer and less sweet than Tokyo's tamagoyaki.
dashimaki_tamago.culture: A measure of a chef's skill; served at sushi counters and as obanzai.
dashimaki_tamago.gf: Egg + dashi + a little soy/mirin — dashi/soy may contain wheat, and it usually has fish dashi.
dashimaki_tamago.vegan: Egg-based with fish dashi — not vegan.

chawanmushi.name: Chawanmushi (茶碗蒸し), literally ’tea-bowl steamed’ — a savory egg custard set gently in a lidded cup. The name records the vessel and the method rather than the contents, a quiet bit of poetry: a dish defined by the bowl it trembles in.
chawanmushi.history: Chawanmushi traces to Nagasaki in the Edo period, born of shippoku cuisine — the Sino-Japanese-Dutch banquet style that flourished around the city’s Chinese quarter when Nagasaki was Japan’s sole window on the world. It descends from Chinese steamed-egg dishes, which Japanese cooks loosened with dashi until the custard arrived at the silky, soup-rich form we know; the famed Nagasaki house Yossou (founded 1866) still serves it as its specialty.
chawanmushi.culture: It is one of world cooking’s rare savory egg custards and, because no chopsticks can lift it, one of the few Japanese dishes eaten with a spoon. Beneath the surface hide small treasures — ginnan (ginkgo nut), kamaboko, shiitake, chicken, shrimp, lily-bulb yuri-ne, a sprig of mitsuba — which is why it sits as easily in a formal kaiseki course as it does as winter comfort food at home.
chawanmushi.taste: Texture is the whole art: the custard should be just-set and barely trembling, smooth as silk with no bubbles or pockmarked holes — the unmistakable proof of patient, low-temperature steaming. It tastes overwhelmingly of clean savory dashi, the egg present only as body, and the pleasure is the slow spoon-dig that turns up a bitter ginkgo nut or a curl of shrimp; served piping hot in cold months, sometimes chilled and trembling in Kyoto’s summer heat.
chawanmushi.gf: An egg-and-dashi custard whose dashi usually carries soy sauce and whose seasoning sometimes leans on wheat-bearing soy, with hidden kamaboko, surimi, or chicken adding further risk — ask about every component before ordering.
chawanmushi.vegan: Not vegan: it is set with egg, built on bonito dashi, and almost always conceals chicken, shrimp, or fish-paste kamaboko within.

agedashi_tofu.history: Lightly fried tofu in a dashi broth — a classic izakaya dish with temple-cuisine roots.
agedashi_tofu.culture: Silky inside, crisp outside; gentle and warming.
agedashi_tofu.gf: Tofu is dusted with potato or wheat starch (varies) and served in dashi-soy broth — confirm; often not GF.
agedashi_tofu.vegan: Usually fish dashi; can be made vegan with kombu dashi — ask.

agedashi_dofu.history: Lightly fried tofu in a dashi broth — a classic izakaya dish with temple-cuisine roots (same as agedashi-tofu).
agedashi_dofu.culture: Silky inside, crisp outside; gentle and warming.
agedashi_dofu.gf: Tofu dusted with potato or wheat starch + dashi-soy broth — confirm; often not GF.
agedashi_dofu.vegan: Usually fish dashi; vegan with kombu dashi — ask.

ganmodoki.history: A deep-fried tofu fritter studded with vegetables — Buddhist-cuisine origins; the name nods to "mock goose."
ganmodoki.culture: Simmered in oden or nimono; hearty and savory.
ganmodoki.gf: May use a yam/flour binder and is simmered in soy-dashi — confirm; not assumed GF.
ganmodoki.vegan: Traditionally vegan (tofu + vegetables), but some add egg and it's often simmered in fish dashi — ask.

fu_dengaku.history: Namafu (生麩) is wheat gluten: wheat flour kneaded and washed under water until only the springy gluten remains, then bound with mochiko (glutinous rice flour) and steamed. The technique is generally traced to roughly the 14th century, arriving from China and embraced by Buddhist monks as a vital protein source under the prohibition on meat — anchoring it in Kyoto’s shōjin and later kaiseki cuisine. Grilled here in the dengaku style, whose name descends from the Heian-era dengaku-mai: stilt-walking field dancers in white hakama whom a single skewer of glazed fu is said to resemble.
fu_dengaku.culture: Kyoto claims the finest namafu, the city’s soft mountain water credited for its texture, and specialist makers like Fuka (麩嘉) and Hanbey (半兵衛麩) have kept the craft for generations. It belongs to the temple kitchen and the kaiseki table alike, often colored and shaped to the season — yomogi-green mugwort, awa millet, leaves cut as maple or ginkgo. Dengaku itself, born sacred, became Edo street food; this version keeps the elegant Kyoto register.
fu_dengaku.taste: The whole point is the bite: namafu is uniquely springy and chewy, dense and mochi-like, almost glutinous — a slow, yielding resistance that dried yakifu’s airy crumb never has. Nearly flavorless on its own, it works as a canvas, lacquered in sweet dengaku miso that catches a faint char on the skewer, so each piece reads as warm caramelized miso against cool elastic chew. Look for the seasonal tints — the herbal lift of yomogi, the nubbly sweetness of awa — and that satisfying tug as you pull it from the bamboo.
fu_dengaku.gf: Not gluten-free — namafu is literally concentrated wheat gluten, and the dengaku miso glaze typically adds more wheat; this is the opposite of safe for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners, so steer clear and always ask.
fu_dengaku.vegan: Namafu plus a sweet miso glaze is usually plant-based and a shōjin temple-cuisine staple, but dengaku miso is sometimes loosened with dashi (bonito) or enriched with egg yolk — ask how the miso is made.

saikyo_yaki.history: The technique descends from misozuke, the old practice of burying perishable foods in miso to preserve them — a real concern in a capital set deep inland, far from any coast. The marinade takes its name from Saikyō, the ‘western capital,’ what Kyoto came to be called after the Meiji court decamped for Tokyo, and the dish crystallized into its refined Edo-period form as preservation slid into pure pleasure. Classically it dresses fatty fish — gindara (black cod) or sawara (Spanish mackerel).
saikyo_yaki.culture: Saikyō miso is Kyoto’s signature white miso: pale gold, heavy on rice koji and unusually low in salt (roughly 5 percent against a standard 12), which is why it reads as sweet rather than briny. That profile was prized at the imperial table and in shōjin temple cooking, and it remains a load-bearing element of washoku’s Kyoto dialect — the same gentle, sugar-forward palate that runs through the city’s confections and its famous white miso zōni at New Year.
saikyo_yaki.taste: Look for a glaze gone deep amber and tacky at the edges, the miso’s sugars lacquered just to the brink of scorching — the cook’s whole art is pulling it before that, because miso burns in a heartbeat. The days-long cure draws out water and firms the flesh, so under the caramelized crust the fish turns dense yet custardy, almost velvet, the koji-sweetness playing against the oily richness of black cod. Hunt for that tension of sweet-savory char and clean, fermented depth; a good one tastes seasoned all the way through, not merely painted.
saikyo_yaki.gf: The cure is miso plus mirin and sake — Saikyō white miso is classically rice-koji based, but barley (mugi) or wheat miso is not gluten-free and mirin/sake/soy add-ins vary by kitchen, so ask before assuming.
saikyo_yaki.vegan: The fish itself is not vegan, but the same Saikyō-miso cure is often used on tofu and vegetables, so ask whether a plant-based version is available.

buta_kakuni.history: Pork belly braised until meltingly tender in soy, sugar, and sake — Nagasaki/Kyushu roots (from Chinese dongpo pork).
buta_kakuni.culture: Rich and indulgent; falls apart at a touch.
buta_kakuni.gf: Braised in soy sauce (wheat) — not GF.

tonjiru.history: A hearty miso soup with pork and root vegetables — warming farmhouse cooking.
tonjiru.culture: A filling side that's almost a meal; winter comfort.
tonjiru.vegan: Pork and fish dashi — not vegan.

tsukune.history: Minced chicken meatballs grilled on skewers (yakitori) or simmered, sometimes with a raw egg-yolk dip.
tsukune.culture: A yakitori favorite — juicy, often glazed with tare.
tsukune.gf: Tare has wheat soy and binders may include flour — usually not GF; salt versions are safer.

negiyaki.history: A thinner, green-onion-heavy cousin of okonomiyaki from Osaka.
negiyaki.culture: Lighter and more savory; griddle comfort food.
negiyaki.vegan: Usually egg, dashi, and often meat — not vegan as standard.

anpan.name: An (餡) is sweet red-bean paste; pan is bread — itself a 16th-century loanword from the Portuguese pão. Anpan is, literally, ‘bean-paste bread’: a Western import wrapped around Japan’s most familiar sweet.
anpan.history: A landmark of Meiji-era invention: in 1874 the founder of Tokyo’s Kimuraya (木村屋), Kimura Yasubei, and his son Eisaburō solved the puzzle of making Western bread palatable to Japanese tongues by leavening the dough not with brewer’s yeast but with sakedane — a starter of rice and kōji borrowed from sake and sweet-bun brewing — then filling it with the familiar comfort of anko. On April 4, 1875, through the swordsman-courtier Yamaoka Tesshū, a version dimpled in the navel with a salt-pickled Yoshino cherry blossom was presented to Emperor Meiji, who asked that it keep being delivered; the date is now marked as Anpan Day.
anpan.culture: The anpan is the seed from which all of Japan’s kashipan (sweet breads) grew — proof that ‘Western’ flour could be made wholly Japanese. By long Kimuraya custom the topping reads the filling: a sesame seed for tsubuan, a poppy seed for koshian, the salted blossom reserved for the imperial sakura version. It remains a near-universal childhood madeleine, immortalized too in the children’s hero Anpanman.
anpan.taste: Look for a thin, almost membranous lacquer-brown crust giving way to a pale, pillowy crumb that carries a faint, sweet-sour whisper of sake from the sakedane — the tell of a real one. The pleasure is in the ratio: a generous, cool, just-sweet dome of anko barely contained by bread, tsubuan offering little bursts of skin and bean against koshian’s silk. Eat it the day it’s baked, while the crumb still springs back and the salted cherry, if present, pricks the sweetness with a clean briny edge.
anpan.gf: Not gluten-free — it is a wheat-flour bread bun; ask.
anpan.vegan: Bread and anko sound vegan, but the dough’s sakedane often rides alongside milk, an egg wash, or butter, so it usually isn’t; ask.

zenzai.history: A warm sweet red-bean soup with mochi — an old comfort sweet, especially in winter; Kansai's name for it.
zenzai.culture: Cozy and nostalgic; served at tea houses and festivals.
zenzai.gf: Azuki and sugar are GF, and mochi is glutinous RICE (GF) — confirm no wheat additives.

yokan.history: A firm jelled sweet of red-bean paste and agar — a centuries-old wagashi, originally a Chinese import.
yokan.culture: Dense and refined; sliced and served with tea, and a classic gift.

wagashi.history: The word splits into wa (Japanese) and kashi → gashi (sweets), and it was coined only at the end of the 1800s to mark Japan’s own confectionery off against the cakes and cookies of the West, the yōgashi pouring in during the Meiji opening; before that, kashi had meant little more than fruit and nuts. Its refinement, though, runs deeper: tea arrived from China by the 12th century, the tea ceremony under Sen no Rikyū demanded sweets worthy of the bowl, and when Portuguese traders brought sugar in the 16th century and Edo-period Japan began producing its own, the art finally spread beyond court and temple into the hands of the people. From that flowering Kyoto’s elegant kyōgashi emerged as a school of its own.
wagashi.culture: Wagashi is the partner to a bowl of matcha, eaten just before the tea so its quiet sweetness cushions the green bitterness to come. The trade sorts itself by moisture — moist namagashi, the in-between han-namagashi, and the dry, long-keeping higashi — and at the pinnacle sit Kyoto’s jō-namagashi, fresh sweets sculpted to a single fleeting moment of the year: a maple leaf, a plum bud, water seeming to flow on a summer afternoon. Each is given a poetic name, often lifted from classical verse or built on a pun, so the confection is read as much as eaten, engaging all five senses before it ever reaches the tongue.
wagashi.taste: Look first, because the eyes are meant to feed before the mouth: the season should be legible in the colour and shape, and the name on the card completes the picture like the last word of a haiku. The sweetness is deliberately restrained and clean, never cloying, holding back so the earthy depth of the azuki and the bitterness of the matcha can lead. Then it is all texture — the bouncy give of mochi, the dense cool smoothness of nerikiri pressed under a bamboo spatula, the brittle melting snap of a higashi — a tiny edible poem you contemplate, then dissolve.
wagashi.gf: It splits both ways: many wagashi are built from rice, beans, and agar and are naturally wheat-free (mochi, yōkan, dango, nerikiri), but plenty rely on wheat — manju and fu-manju skins, dorayaki, monaka wafers — and cross-contamination plus additives in the anko vary by maker, so ask before assuming.
wagashi.vegan: Traditional wagashi is unusually vegan-friendly for a sweet, leaning on beans, rice, sugar, and agar (a seaweed gel, not gelatin), but watch for the exceptions — egg in castella-style sweets, honey in some glazes and fillings — so ask.

sashimi.history: The name is a quiet riddle: sashi-mi reads as ‘pierced flesh,’ not ‘cut flesh,’ and two stories explain why. One holds that cooks once pinned a sliver of the fish’s own fin to the slices so diners could tell what they were eating; the other, that in the warrior age the word kiru (‘to cut’) was inauspicious for samurai, so sasu (‘to pierce’) took its place. Either way the dish itself is ancient — its lineage runs back through namasu, the thinly sliced raw fish dressed in vinegar that the Heian court already prized — making ‘cut, not cooked’ Japan’s oldest way of honoring a fish.
sashimi.culture: Sashimi is the cuisine’s purest argument that cooking can be subtraction. Everything rides on the knife — the long willow-leaf yanagiba — and the single drawn stroke (hiki-giri) that leaves a mirror-smooth cut face; lean it flat for hira-zukuri, whisper-thin for usu-zukuri on white fish you can read a newspaper through. The tsuma — shredded daikon, a leaf of shiso, the dab of wasabi — are not garnish but grammar: they refresh the palate, cut richness, and once helped guard against spoilage. In landlocked Kyoto this restraint carried real weight, for the old capital lived on preserved sea fish — salted saba hauled down the Saba Kaidō from Wakasa, summer hamo — which made a plate of genuinely fresh sashimi a particular and pointed luxury.
sashimi.taste: Read the fish before you taste it: the slices should have a clean sheen and a firm, alive spring, each cut face flat and glassy from one stroke, never frayed or sawn. Let it come up off ice toward cool-not-cold so the flavor opens, then taste the range across the plate — lean akami clean and iron-bright, fatty toro melting at body heat, springy white fish almost sweet, the snap of squid or the brine of scallop. Be sparing: a bare drag of soy on the fish (not the rice-paper underside), a trace of wasabi pressed onto the slice rather than stirred into the dish, so the fish, not the seasoning, is what you remember.
sashimi.gf: Fish is naturally gluten-free; the trap is the soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat — bring or request tamari, or eat it with a pinch of salt and yuzu instead, and double-check that wasabi is real grated root rather than a wheat-bound paste; always ask.
sashimi.vegan: No — sashimi is by definition raw fish and seafood, the antithesis of plant-based.
